title: Cat Seizures and Convulsions: Emergency Triage and Care Guide
meta_desc: Learn how to recognize cat seizures, why they require high-urgency veterinary care, and how to document episodes to help your vet diagnose the cause.
content_html:
TL;DR: A seizure in a cat is a high-priority medical emergency that requires immediate veterinary attention if the episode lasts more than two minutes or occurs multiple times. Capturing a video of the event is a vital diagnostic tool to help your veterinarian identify the underlying cause.

What does it look like when a cat has a seizure or convulsion?

A seizure is a sudden, uncontrolled electrical disturbance in your cat's brain. This can result in localized twitching (focal seizures) or full-body convulsions (generalized seizures) where the cat may collapse, lose consciousness, and exhibit paddling movements with their legs. It is a frightening experience for any pet owner, but remaining calm is the best way you can support your pet during this time.

Is a seizure in my cat a medical emergency?

Any seizure activity is considered a high-priority medical emergency. While a single, brief seizure may not be immediately fatal, it is often a symptom of an underlying health issue such as toxins, metabolic disease, or neurological conditions. You should seek veterinary care immediately if:

  • The seizure lasts more than two minutes.
  • Your cat has multiple seizures in a row.
  • This is the first time your cat has ever experienced a convulsion.

Why is it helpful to provide my veterinarian with a video of the seizure?

While it is difficult to think about filming during such a stressful moment, a video is one of the most valuable tools you can provide to a veterinarian for several reasons:

  • Seizures often stop by the time you arrive at the clinic, and a video provides visual evidence of the exact nature of the movements.
  • It allows the vet to accurately evaluate the duration and severity of the episode.
  • It helps distinguish between a true seizure and other conditions like fainting, inner ear issues, or heart problems, ensuring a more rapid diagnosis.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats experiencing seizures, diazepam can be administered rectally at 1 mg/kg as an initial intervention. For uncontrolled seizures, diazepam at 0.5-1 mg/kg PO bid may be considered, but close monitoring for potential fatal hepatic necrosis is warranted during the first few weeks of therapy. Phenobarbital is the anticonvulsant of choice in cats; if seizures remain uncontrolled, levetiracetam can be added. In status epilepticus, diazepam can be given as a constant-rate infusion at 0.5-2 mg/kg/hr. If phenobarbital is contraindicated due to hepatic conditions, levetiracetam at 40-60 mg/kg IV, SC, or rectally can be administered. Recommended maintenance anticonvulsant therapy includes phenobarbital at 2-4 mg/kg PO bid, adjusted to control seizures or maintain serum levels at 15-40 mcg/mL.

Chapter: Neurology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1221)

faqs.question: What should I do while my cat is convulsing?
faqs.answer: Clear away any furniture or sharp objects to prevent injury, but do not try to restrain your cat or put your hands near their mouth, as they may bite involuntarily.

faqs.question: Are seizures painful for cats?
faqs.answer: Generally, seizures are not painful, though the cat is unconscious and unaware of their surroundings. They may feel confused or anxious during the recovery phase.

faqs.question: What happens after the seizure stops?
faqs.answer: Your cat will enter the 'post-ictal' phase, where they may appear disoriented, wobbly, or even temporarily blind. Keep them in a safe, quiet area until you can get them to a veterinarian.
